comment_text.sn7.11:1.1: This discourse recurs at Snp 1.4 with a longer narrative. Vedic hymns include invocations to the “Lord of the Field” to bless the plowing and the crops (Rig Veda 4.57.4), but it is unorthodox to see a brahmin farmer.
comment_text.sn7.11:1.2: The Southern Hills (dakkhiṇāgiri, “Deccan”) is the Vindhya Range that lies on the southern border of Magadha, leading southwest to Avanti. The Buddha and his disciples visited there so rarely that people complained (Kd 1:53.1.3). | Ekanāḷa (“one-stalk”) appears only here.
comment_text.sn7.11:2.1: Atharvaveda 6.142.3 says that the “givers” of the grain shall be inexhaustible, perhaps suggesting a food distribution practice.
comment_text.sn7.11:4.1: Vedic texts are full of this sort of correspondence, but I cannot find a close match to these. Perhaps this is to be expected, as the farmer is learning something new.
comment_text.sn7.11:6.2: Yogakkhema (“sanctuary from the yoke”) is a metaphor for Nibbana that draws from the literal sense of khema as an oasis or sanctuary for men and beasts. After a hard day’s journey, a caravan would stop to rest, “unyoke” the draft animals, lay down the burden, and refresh themselves in the cool waters.

root_text.sn7.11:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā magadhesu viharati dakkhiṇāgirismiṁ ekanāḷāyaṁ brāhmaṇagāme.
root_text.sn7.11:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena kasibhāradvājassa brāhmaṇassa pañcamattāni naṅgalasatāni payuttāni honti vappakāle.
root_text.sn7.11:1.4: Atha kho bhagavā pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya yena kasibhāradvājassa brāhmaṇassa kammanto tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn7.11:2.1: Tena kho pana samayena kasibhāradvājassa brāhmaṇassa parivesanā vattati.
root_text.sn7.11:2.2: Atha kho bhagavā yena parivesanā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhāsi.
root_text.sn7.11:2.3: Addasā kho kasibhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ piṇḍāya ṭhitaṁ.
root_text.sn7.11:2.5: “ahaṁ kho, samaṇa, kasāmi ca vapāmi ca, kasitvā ca vapitvā ca bhuñjāmi.
root_text.sn7.11:2.6: Tvampi, samaṇa, kasassu ca vapassu ca, kasitvā ca vapitvā ca bhuñjassū”ti.
root_text.sn7.11:2.7: “Ahampi kho, brāhmaṇa, kasāmi ca vapāmi ca, kasitvā ca vapitvā ca bhuñjāmī”ti.
root_text.sn7.11:2.8: “Na kho mayaṁ passāma bhoto gotamassa yugaṁ vā naṅgalaṁ vā phālaṁ vā pācanaṁ vā balībadde vā, atha ca pana bhavaṁ gotamo evamāha:
root_text.sn7.11:2.9: ‘ahampi kho, brāhmaṇa, kasāmi ca vapāmi ca, kasitvā ca vapitvā ca bhuñjāmī’”ti.
root_text.sn7.11:2.10: Atha kho kasibhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn7.11:8.3: Yañhi bhavaṁ gotamo amatapphalampi kasiṁ kasatī”ti.
root_text.sn7.11:11.1: Evaṁ vutte, kasibhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:

translation_text.sn7.11:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Magadhans in the Southern Hills near the brahmin village of Ekanāḷa.
translation_text.sn7.11:1.3: Now at that time the brahmin Bhāradvāja the Farmer had harnessed around five hundred plows, it being the season for sowing.
translation_text.sn7.11:1.4: Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to where Bhāradvāja the Farmer was working.
translation_text.sn7.11:2.1: Now at that time Bhāradvāja the Farmer was distributing food.
translation_text.sn7.11:2.2: Then the Buddha went to where the distribution was taking place and stood to one side.
translation_text.sn7.11:2.3: Bhāradvāja the Farmer saw him standing for alms
translation_text.sn7.11:2.5: “I plough and sow, ascetic, and then I eat.
translation_text.sn7.11:2.6: You too should plough and sow, then you may eat.”
translation_text.sn7.11:2.7: “I too plough and sow, brahmin, and then I eat.”
translation_text.sn7.11:2.8: “I don’t see the worthy Gotama with a yoke or plow or plowshare or goad or oxen, yet he says:
translation_text.sn7.11:2.9: ‘I too plough and sow, brahmin, and then I eat.’”
translation_text.sn7.11:2.10: Then Bhāradvāja the Farmer addressed the Buddha in verse:
translation_text.sn7.11:3.2: but I don’t see you farming.
translation_text.sn7.11:3.3: Tell me how you’re a farmer when asked:
translation_text.sn7.11:3.4: how am I to recognize your farming?”
translation_text.sn7.11:4.1: “Faith is my seed, fervor my rain,
translation_text.sn7.11:4.2: and wisdom is my yoke and plough.
translation_text.sn7.11:4.3: Conscience is my pole, mind my strap,
translation_text.sn7.11:5.2: I restrict my intake of food.
translation_text.sn7.11:5.3: I use truth as my scythe,
translation_text.sn7.11:6.1: Energy is my beast of burden,
translation_text.sn7.11:6.2: transporting me to sanctuary from the yoke.
translation_text.sn7.11:6.4: to where there is no sorrow.
translation_text.sn7.11:7.1: That’s how to do the farming
translation_text.sn7.11:7.2: that has freedom from death as its fruit.
translation_text.sn7.11:8.2: The worthy one is a farmer.
translation_text.sn7.11:8.3: For the worthy Gotama does the farming that has freedom from death as its fruit.”
translation_text.sn7.11:9.1: “Food enchanted by a verse isn’t fit for me to eat.
translation_text.sn7.11:9.2: That’s not the principle of those who see, brahmin.
translation_text.sn7.11:9.3: The Buddhas cast aside things enchanted with verses.
translation_text.sn7.11:9.4: Since there is such a principle, brahmin, <j>that’s how they live.
translation_text.sn7.11:10.1: Serve with other food and drink
translation_text.sn7.11:10.2: the consummate one, the great seer,
translation_text.sn7.11:10.3: with defilements ended and remorse stilled.
translation_text.sn7.11:10.4: For he is the field for the seeker of merit.”
translation_text.sn7.11:11.1: When he had spoken, the brahmin Bhāradvāja the Farmer said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.11:11.3: From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”
